title: Product Records — Structs
description: Define a Product struct with fields: name (string), price (double), quantity (int), and category (string). Replace the three parallel arrays with a single dynamically allocated array of Product structs. Update all functions to work with the Product struct, using the dot operator to access fields. Assign your C++ code to the variable `cpp`.
test_code:
assert 'struct' in cpp, 'Define a Product struct'
assert 'Product' in cpp, 'The struct should be named Product'
assert '.name' in cpp, 'Access the name field using the dot operator'
assert '.price' in cpp, 'Access the price field using the dot operator'
assert '.quantity' in cpp or '.qty' in cpp, 'Access the quantity field using the dot operator'
assert '.category' in cpp or '.cat' in cpp, 'Include a category field in the Product struct'
assert 'Product products[]' in cpp or 'Product *products' in cpp or 'Product* products' in cpp, 'Use an array or pointer of Product structs'
assert cpp.count('.name') >= 2, 'Use the dot operator to access struct fields in multiple places'
acceptance_criteria: Product struct defined with name (string), price (double), quantity (int), category (string)
Single array of Product structs replaces the three parallel arrays
All functions updated to accept Product array instead of separate arrays
Dot operator used to access struct fields (products[i].name, etc.)
Category field is read from user and included in display and file output
hints: Define the struct above main: struct Product { string name; double price; int quantity; string category; };
Swapping structs is simpler than parallel arrays: just swap one Product variable
The dot operator accesses fields: products[i].name, products[i].price
Functions now take Product products[] instead of three separate arrays
